payload.wording: AZA-accredited zoos do more good than harm — net positives for conservation, public understanding, and individual animals.
Modern accredited zoos = AZA-accredited institutions and international equivalents; roadside menageries and unaccredited operations are out of scope.
Modern accredited zoos, on balance, do more good than harm.
Modern accredited zoos are a net positive for conservation.
Modern accredited zoos are a net positive for public understanding of wildlife.
Modern accredited zoos are a net positive for the individual animals in their care.

payload.content: Scope-accepted; the accredited/unaccredited line does argumentative work, narrowing what Anna defends and what Ben may cite.

proposal.content: Scope-accepted; the boundary does argumentative work.

payload.wording: Confinement of wide-ranging, cognitively complex animals imposes a morally significant cost independent of welfare outcomes.
Confinement-as-such imposes a morally significant cost on the individual.
That cost is independent of measurable welfare outcomes and must enter the netting against benefits.
Welfare science plus revealed-preference data converge on no remaining unmet interest in well-managed captives.
Frustration of capacities constitutive of a kind is a moral cost even when welfare metrics are acceptable.
The species at issue have cognitive/ecological profiles organized around ranging, foraging, and social navigation such that confinement frustrates those capacities.
A creature has a form of flourishing proper to its kind; frustrating constitutive capacities is harm independent of subjective welfare.
A life has a shape it is owed; thwarting that shape counts morally.
The notion of 'kind-constitutive capacities' fails to survive philosophy-of-biology scrutiny.
Capability-frustration reduces without remainder to welfare deficits in every case.

payload.content: Shared axiom — both debaters hold N12 as bedrock from their respective frames. The disagreement is no longer whether lives have shapes that can be wronged — it is how we know which shapes, and what follows.

proposal.content: Shared axiom — both debaters hold N12 as bedrock.

payload.wording: Our evidence that a capacity is constitutive is almost always that frustrating it produces suffering, stereotypy, shortened lives, or developmental failure.

proposal.new_wording: Our evidence that a capacity is constitutive is, in nearly all cases, that frustrating it produces welfare deficits.

payload.wording: Welfare deficits are our evidence for constitutive capacities (epistemic reading).
Capability-frustration just IS welfare loss, ontologically (metaphysical reading).

payload.content: If welfare is the access to constitutive capacities, then the husbandry-netting question is the operational form of the constitutive-capacities dispute, not a sidestep of it.

proposal.content: If welfare is the access to constitutive capacities, then the husbandry-netting question is the operational form of the constitutive-capacities dispute, not a sidestep of it.

payload.wording: Candidate constitutive-but-welfare-neutral capacities exist (hatchery salmon, wolf-pack structure, bonsai-developmental), but each is contestable.

proposal.wording: Candidate constitutive-but-welfare-neutral capacities exist (hatchery salmon, wolf-pack structure, bonsai-developmental), but each is contestable.

payload.content: Decline to press despite available concession; methodological, not substantive.

payload.wording: What is owed — that a life has a shape it is owed — precedes welfare-aggregation; the netting question is downstream of that priority, not its operational form.
